questions.question: A student performs three actions with a magnet and a solenoid connected to a galvanometer. Which row in the table correctly predicts the observations?nnAction | Observationn--- | ---n1. Magnet moving in | Deflectionn2. Magnet stationary inside | No deflectionn3. Magnet moving out | Opposite deflection
options: All three are correct
1 and 2 only
2 and 3 only
1 and 3 only
questions.explanation:
These observations are standard for electromagnetic induction. Deflection only occurs during relative motion, and reversing the motion reverses the deflection.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A magnet is moved towards a coil of wire. Which of the following does NOT increase the size of the induced e.m.f.?
options: Using a thicker wire with lower resistance
Moving the magnet more quickly
Using a stronger magnet
Increasing the number of turns in the coil
questions.explanation:
While thicker wire may increase the induced current ($I$), it does not affect the magnitude of the induced e.m.f. ($V$), which depends on flux and turns.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The diagram shows the North pole of a magnet moving towards a solenoid.n[N-S Magnet] $rightarrow$ [Solenoid End A]nWhat is the polarity induced at End A and what is the nature of the force between them?
options: Polarity: North; Force: Repulsion
Polarity: South; Force: Attraction
Polarity: North; Force: Attraction
Polarity: South; Force: Repulsion
questions.explanation:
To oppose the North pole coming towards it, End A must become a North pole. Like poles (N and N) repel each other.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The graph shows the induced e.m.f. in a coil over time. Which feature of the graph indicates that the magnet was moving faster as it left the coil than when it entered?n[Graph shows a small first peak and a tall second peak in the opposite direction]
options: The height of the second peak is greater than the first
The second peak is in the opposite direction to the first
The area under the two peaks is the same
The time duration for both peaks is identical
questions.explanation:
Induced e.m.f. magnitude is proportional to speed. A taller peak on the graph indicates a higher induced e.m.f., which implies the magnet was moving faster at that time.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A wire is moved upwards through a horizontal magnetic field that points from North to South. In which direction is the current induced in the wire? (Hint: Use Fleming's right-hand rule)
options: Out of the page
Into the page
Towards the North pole
Towards the South pole
questions.explanation:
Using Fleming's Right-Hand Rule: Thumb (Motion) up, First finger (Field) N to S (right), Second finger (Current) points out of the page.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: An a.c. generator is connected to an oscilloscope. The trace shows a peak voltage of $2\text{ V}$ and a period of $0.04\text{ s}$. What is the frequency of the a.c. signal?
options: $25\text{ Hz}$
$50\text{ Hz}$
$0.04\text{ Hz}$
$2\text{ Hz}$
questions.explanation:
Frequency $f = 1/T = 1 / 0.04 = 25\text{ Hz}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the voltage value ($2\text{ V}$) in the frequency calculation.

questions.question: Which of the following procedures does NOT generate an induced e.m.f. in a coil of wire?
options: Holding a magnet stationary inside the coil
Rotating the coil in a magnetic field
Rotating a magnet around the stationary coil
Moving a bar magnet towards the coil
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Question 6, holding a magnet stationary results in no change in magnetic flux, so no e.m.f. is induced. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking being 'inside' a magnetic field is enough, regardless of motion.

questions.question: In electromagnetic induction, which of the following factors does NOT affect the magnitude of the induced e.m.f.?
options: The resistance of the coil
The strength of the magnetic field
The speed of the coil's motion
The number of turns in the coil
questions.explanation:
Directly from Review Question 7. Resistance affects the current ($I = V/R$), but the induced e.m.f. ($V$) depends only on the rate of cutting field lines. 

**Common mistake:** Confusing e.m.f. (voltage) with the resulting current.

questions.question: The diagram shows a graph of induced e.m.f. against time for a generator. Which change was made to the generator to produce the second curve?nn[Scenario: Curve 2 has half the period and twice the peak voltage of Curve 1]
options: The frequency of rotation was doubled.
The number of turns in the coil was doubled.
The magnetic field strength was doubled.
The resistance of the external circuit was halved.
questions.explanation:
According to Worked Example 18B and Figure 18.11, doubling the frequency of rotation ($f$) results in both doubling the peak voltage and halving the period ($T = 1/f$). 

**Common mistake:** Choosing 'doubling turns', which would increase voltage but not change the period.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly describes the function of slip rings in an a.c. generator?
options: They provide a continuous connection between the rotating coil and the fixed brushes.
They reverse the current direction every half-turn of the coil.
They convert the induced a.c. into a d.c. output.
They increase the magnetic field strength of the permanent magnets.
questions.explanation:
Slip rings maintain electrical contact with the brushes while the coil rotates, without twisting the wires. 

**Common mistake:** Attributing the current-reversal function to slip rings; that is the role of a commutator in a motor.

questions.question: The diagram shows a solenoid connected to a d.c. power supply.n[Diagram: Coil with current flowing N-pole at left]nWhich row describes the magnetic field at the ends of the solenoid?
options: Left end: North pole; Right end: South pole
Left end: South pole; Right end: North pole
Left end: North pole; Right end: North pole
Both ends are South poles
questions.explanation:
Using the right-hand grip rule for solenoids, the direction of current in the coils determines the North and South poles at the ends.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student builds a magnetic relay using a coil, a magnet (M), an armature (L), and a stopper (S). Which combination of materials is best for M, L, and S?n(Relay diagram shown in Figure 15.20)
options: M: Iron; L: Iron; S: Rubber
M: Iron; L: Copper; S: Rubber
M: Plastic; L: Iron; S: Rubber
M: Iron; L: Iron; S: Iron
questions.explanation:
Based on Let's Review Section A Q3, M and L should be iron (magnetically soft) to allow for switching, and S should be rubber to cushion the impact.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT affect the strength of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire?
options: The thickness of the insulation on the wire
The magnitude of the current
The distance from the wire
The medium surrounding the wire
questions.explanation:
Field strength depends on current and distance. Insulation thickness does not change the magnetic effect of the current inside the conductor.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A plotting compass is placed near a wire carrying a large direct current. The compass needle points in a specific direction. What happens when the current is increased?
options: The needle points more firmly in the same direction
The needle reverses direction
The needle begins to spin rapidly
The needle points toward the wire
questions.explanation:
Increasing the current increases the strength of the magnetic field, which exerts a stronger force to align the compass needle, but it does not change the field's direction.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The diagram shows a cross-section of a wire with current flowing 'into the page' ($otimes$). Which diagram correctly shows the magnetic field lines?n[A: Clockwise circles; B: Anticlockwise circles; C: Radial lines out; D: Radial lines in]
options: C
D
questions.explanation:
Using the right-hand grip rule: point your thumb into the page (direction of current). Your fingers curl clockwise, representing the magnetic field lines.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: The diagram shows a current-carrying wire passing through a magnetic field. In which direction does the wire move?nn[Diagram: N-pole on left, S-pole on right, current arrow pointing into the page]
options: Towards the bottom of the page
Towards the top of the page
Towards the N-pole
Towards the S-pole
questions.explanation:
This is based on Review Section A, Question 2. Field is left to right, current is into the page. Fleming's left-hand rule gives a force towards the bottom. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the wire is attracted to one of the poles.

questions.question: A beam of electrons enters a magnetic field that is directed into the page. What is the initial direction of the deflection of the electrons?nn[Reference: Figure 18.51]
options: Towards the top of the page
Towards the bottom of the page
Into the page
Out of the page
questions.explanation:
Directly from Review Question 4. For an electron beam moving right, conventional current is left. Field is into the page. FLHR: Field (index) in, Current (middle) left, Thumb points up. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting 'bottom of the page' by not reversing current for electrons.

questions.question: A student has two parallel wires. When a current flows through them in the same direction, they attract. What happens if the current in one of the wires is reversed?
options: The wires repel each other.
The wires still attract each other.
The force between them becomes zero.
The wires start to rotate.
questions.explanation:
If currents in the same direction attract, reversing one current creates the 'opposite' magnetic configuration, leading to repulsion. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking they still attract because they are both 'magnetic'.

questions.question: A current-carrying coil is placed in a magnetic field as shown. In which direction do forces act on the sides of the coil to make it move?nn[Reference: Figure 18.50]
options: Upwards on one side and downwards on the other, causing rotation.
Both sides are pushed outwards, expanding the coil.
The entire coil is pushed out of the magnetic field.
Both sides are pushed towards the axis XY.
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Question 3. A coil in a magnetic field experiences opposite forces on its two sides (due to opposite current directions relative to the field), creating a turning effect. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the whole coil moves in one linear direction.

questions.question: Which change to a d.c. motor would NOT increase the turning effect on the coil?
options: Reversing the direction of the current
Increasing the number of turns in the coil
Increasing the magnitude of the current
Using a stronger permanent magnet
questions.explanation:
Reversing current changes the direction of the turning effect but not its magnitude. The other options all increase the force. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking reversal always counts as an 'increase' in some property.

questions.question: The diagram shows a simple d.c. motor. Which part is the split-ring commutator?n[Diagram showing magnets, coil, and four labelled parts A, B, C, D]
options: The split ring connected to the coil ends
The permanent magnets on the sides
The rectangular coil of wire
The carbon brushes touching the ring
questions.explanation:
Based on standard IGCSE diagrams (like Fig 18.56), the split-ring commutator is the circular ring divided into two halves that rotates with the coil.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student tests a motor and records the following data:nnTrial | Current / A | Resultn--- | --- | ---n1 | 1.0 | Rotates slowlyn2 | 2.0 | Rotates fastern3 | 0.0 | StationarynnWhat can be concluded from this data?
options: The speed of rotation increases as the current increases
The motor requires a minimum of 2.0 A to function
The direction of rotation depends on the current magnitude
The current has no effect on the mechanical energy output
questions.explanation:
The data clearly shows that increasing the current from 1.0 A to 2.0 A results in a faster rotation speed, supporting the theory that current affects the magnitude of the force.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: In a simple d.c. motor, the direction of the current in the motor coil is reversed every half-revolution. Which part of the motor enables this?
options: Split-ring commutator
Brushes
Coil
Permanent magnets
questions.explanation:
This is a direct question from the review section. The split-ring commutator is specifically designed to reverse current every 180 degrees of rotation.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which row correctly identifies how to reverse the direction of the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field?
options: Reverse the magnetic field OR reverse the current
Reverse the magnetic field AND reverse the current
Increase the current and keep field the same
Decrease the current and reverse the field
questions.explanation:
To reverse the direction of the force, only one factor (current or field) must be reversed. Reversing both would result in the same original force direction.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A d.c. motor is used in a toy drone. Why are permanent magnets used in the motor?
options: To provide a constant magnetic field for the coil to interact with
To ensure the motor always spins in an anticlockwise direction
To convert kinetic energy back into electrical energy for the battery
To act as a safety switch that breaks the circuit if it gets too hot
questions.explanation:
Permanent magnets provide the external magnetic field necessary for the current-carrying coil to experience a force (the motor effect).

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A transformer has^400 turns in the primary coil and^10 turns in the secondary coil. If the primary voltage is $250\text{ V}$, what is the secondary voltage?
options: $25\text{ V}$
questions.explanation:
Directly from Review Question 7(a)(i). $V_s = V_p \times (N_s / N_p) = 250 \times (10 / 400) = 6.25\text{ V}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying turns ($400 \times 10$).

questions.question: Referring to the transformer in the previous question ($N_p=400, N_s=10, V_p=250\text{V}$), if the primary current is $2.0\text{ A}$, what is the secondary current assuming $100\%$ efficiency?
options: $80\text{ A}$
questions.explanation:
Directly from Review Question 7(a)(ii). $I_s = (I_p \times V_p) / V_s = (2.0 \times 250) / 6.25 = 80\text{ A}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing the primary current by the turns ratio incorrectly.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies properties of an ideal step-up transformer?nn| | Potential Difference | Number of Turns | Current |n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |n| A | $V_s > V_p  | N_s > N_p  | I_s < I_p  |n| B | V_s > V_p  | N_s < N_p  | I_s > I_p  |n| C | V_s < V_p  | N_s > N_p  | I_s < I_p  |n| D | V_s < V_p  | N_s < N_p  | I_s > I_p$ |
options: Row A
Row B
Row C
Row D
questions.explanation:
In a step-up transformer, voltage increases ($V_s > V_p ), meaning turns must increase (N_s > N_p ). For an ideal transformer, current must decrease (I_s < I_p$) to keep power constant. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking everything increases in a 'step-up' transformer.

questions.question: $A student winds a wire^30 \times around a soft iron C-core. To show electromagnetic induction without using a magnet, they should use a second identical C-core and:$
options: Connect one coil to an a.c. supply and the other to a galvanometer.
Connect both coils in series to a high-voltage d.c. battery.
Rub both cores with silk to create static charges.
Place the two cores parallel to each other without touching.
questions.explanation:
Based on Review Question 11. By using two coils on iron cores (forming a transformer) and an a.c. supply, a changing magnetic field is created that induces a current in the second coil. 

**Common mistake:** Using a d.c. battery, which will only induce a current at the moment the switch is closed/opened.

questions.question: The diagram shows a simple iron-cored transformer. Which labels correctly identify parts X and Y?nn[Diagram: Coil X connected to a.c. input, Coil Y connected to a lamp, both on an iron ring]
options: X: Primary coil; Y: Secondary coil
X: Secondary coil; Y: Primary coil
X: Brushes; Y: Slip rings
X: Armature; Y: Commutator
questions.explanation:
In transformer diagrams, the input side is the primary (X) and the output/load side is the secondary (Y). 

**Common mistake:** Swapping the terms or using motor component names.
